one_line_takeaway: The first systematic benchmark of machine unlearning on Vision Transformers finds that CNN-derived proxies and algorithms largely transfer, but method rankings flip by architecture (ViT favors Fine-tune, Swin-T favors NegGrad+) and SalUn is unreliable against MIA-style forget-quality checks in VTs.
key_finding: VTs and CNNs exhibit fundamentally similar (long-tailed) memorization distributions, and simple CNN-derived memorization proxies (especially Confidence and Holdout Retraining) remain valid predictors of memorization in VTs -- Confidence achieves Spearman correlations of -0.79 to -0.91 across VT/dataset combinations, closely matching CNN magnitudes. CNN-derived MU algorithms transfer well to VTs and can match or exceed CNN performance, but method rankings do not transfer directly: NegGrad+ is the most robust algorithm overall (best paired with the Holdout Retraining proxy), especially on more complex datasets and on Swin-T, while Fine-tune performs particularly well on ViT (e.g., ToW-MIA=0.919 on CIFAR-10, 0.831 on CIFAR-100 for ViT with Fine-tune) due to its global-attention/diffuse-parameter structure. SalUn attains good ToW scores but is notably weak on ToW-MIA in VTs, especially ViT-Small (e.g., ToW-MIA=0.582 on CIFAR-10 with the Confidence proxy), indicating it does not reliably defend against membership-inference-style probing of forget-set examples even though it removes forget-set accuracy well. VT pretraining gives a large forget-set-retention advantage on simpler datasets (retrained theta_r reaches >90% accuracy on the CIFAR-10 forget set for ViT/Swin-T vs. ~50% for ResNet-18) but this advantage shrinks as dataset complexity grows (CIFAR-100, ImageNet-1K). Continual unlearning (5 and 10 sequential steps) shows minimal to no cumulative degradation for the best algorithm-proxy pairing (NegGrad+ with Holdout Retraining) across ViT-Small and Swin-Tiny.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic benchmark of machine unlearning (MU) algorithms on Vision Transformers (ViT and Swin-T families), rather than proposing a new unlearning method. It evaluates three CNN-derived MU algorithms (Fine-tune, NegGrad+, SalUn), each instantiated within the memorization-aware RUM framework, across two VT architecture families at multiple capacities, four image classification datasets of varying scale/complexity (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, ImageNet-1K), and both single-shot and continual (5- and 10-step) unlearning protocols. En route it also characterizes how VTs memorize training data relative to CNNs and evaluates five memorization proxies (Confidence, MaxConf, Entropy, Binary Accuracy, Holdout Retraining) for their fidelity as substitutes for the expensive Feldman memorization score in the VT setting.
threat_model: None. This is an empirical benchmarking/measurement paper about machine unlearning efficacy and stability, not an adversarial-security paper. It does not define an attacker, attack surface, or defense goal. Membership Inference Attack (MIA) machinery is used only as an internal evaluation component (the ToW-MIA metric trains a binary in/out classifier on loss values to measure 'forget quality'), not as a studied threat or vulnerability of a system. Forcing a security threat-model framing onto this paper would misrepresent its purpose.
limitations: The paper's own scope constraints, stated or evident from the text: (1) only three MU algorithm families were evaluated (Fine-tune, NegGrad+, SalUn), each wrapped in RUM, not a broad leaderboard of the field's many MU methods (explicitly contrasted with prior 'leaderboard-style' benchmarks); (2) results are averaged over only three runs; (3) capacity-scaling experiments on ImageNet-1K use only Swin-Small/Swin-Base/ViT-Base/16 with the Holdout Retraining proxy, not the full proxy set; (4) the retrain-from-scratch reference model theta_r is itself an approximation used as the unlearning 'gold standard,' and absolute ToW/ToW-MIA values are shown to be sensitive to how well theta_r's own forget-set accuracy is estimated; (5) continual unlearning is tested only for the single best-performing algorithm-proxy pair (NegGrad+ with HR), not for all algorithms, so conclusions about continual-unlearning stability do not necessarily generalize to Fine-tune or SalUn.

quantitative_results: Confidence proxy Spearman correlation with Feldman memorization score: -0.79 to -0.91 across ViT-Small/Swin-Tiny on CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100 (vs. -0.80/-0.91 for ResNet-18/ResNet-50)
Holdout Retraining proxy Spearman correlation: +0.45 to +0.67 across architectures/datasets
Retrained theta_r forget-set accuracy on CIFAR-10: ViT-Small 94.089%, Swin-Tiny 91.867% (Confidence proxy) vs. ResNet-18 50.433%
Swin-Tiny + NegGrad+ + Holdout Retraining (CIFAR-100): ToW = 0.975, ToW-MIA = 0.902
Capacity study (CIFAR-10, HR proxy): NegGrad+ ToW = 0.916 (ViT-Small), 0.957 (ViT-Tiny), 0.944 (Swin-Small), 0.977 (Swin-Tiny)
ImageNet-1K (Swin-Small, HR proxy): NegGrad+ ToW=0.819/ToW-MIA=0.772; Fine-tune ToW=0.780/ToW-MIA=0.747; SalUn ToW=0.743/ToW-MIA=0.647
ImageNet-1K scaling (HR proxy): NegGrad+ ToW rises from 0.819 (Swin-Small, 49M) to 0.837 (Swin-Base, 88M) to 0.842 (ViT-Base/16, 85M)
baselines_compared: RUM framework wrapping each of the above
Retrained-from-scratch model theta_r as gold-standard reference
ResNet-18/ResNet-50 CNN counterparts for cross-architecture comparison
